meta.title: Why Young Men Are Becoming Eastern Orthodox
meta.author: Gavin Ortlund

questions.question: According to the survey cited via the New York Post article, U.S. Orthodox converts in 2022 were up by what percentage compared with pre-pandemic 2019 levels?
Ortlund says the main goal of this video is best described as:
options: A detailed refutation of Eastern Orthodox christology
A debate transcript with an Orthodox priest
A history of the seven ecumenical councils
A shorter, pastoral word to Protestants—especially evangelicals—to pay attention and learn
questions.answer: A shorter, pastoral word to Protestants—especially evangelicals—to pay attention and learn

questions.question: Which three questions structure the talk?
options: Why is this happening, why does it matter, what should we do
questions.answer: Why is this happening, why does it matter, what should we do

questions.question: The first major factor Ortlund draws from the article for why people convert is:
options: The search for stability, tradition, and rootedness
Interest in Byzantine art history only
Disagreement over the date of Easter
questions.answer: The search for stability, tradition, and rootedness

questions.question: The Anglican-background convert is quoted as especially troubled by:
options: A sense that there was no way to stop change—even in one lifetime—and powerlessness before cultural drift
questions.answer: A sense that there was no way to stop change—even in one lifetime—and powerlessness before cultural drift

questions.question: Ortlund links the rock-versus-sand imagery in the article to which book title?
Who wrote Rock and Sand in Ortlund’s account (Orthodox priest featured in the article)?
The second major factor—rigor and related themes—includes converts comparing former Protestant sermons to:
Converts in the article compared some former Protestant worship to:
Jordan Peterson is quoted comparing criticizing an Orthodox service to:
options: Going to a ballet and asking what is going on
questions.answer: Going to a ballet and asking what is going on

questions.question: Ortlund agrees that Protestant settings often place more focal pressure on:
The phenomenon described as people discovering Orthodoxy through online argument and then visiting a parish is called:
The catechist’s parish associated with the article is placed roughly in which area?
Ortlund cites which platforms as shaping major life decisions (for better or worse)?
options: YouTube, podcasts, social media, even TikTok
questions.answer: YouTube, podcasts, social media, even TikTok

questions.question: Ortlund gives as a naive parental reaction:
options: Demanding they read only the Church Fathers
“At least they’re taking their faith seriously” when a child becomes Eastern Orthodox
questions.answer: “At least they’re taking their faith seriously” when a child becomes Eastern Orthodox

questions.question: From Rock and Sand (page 153), Trenham’s exercise for Protestant inquirers includes listing Protestant baggage under which paired columns?
Trenham warns some inquirers may misread conversion as a mere denominational switch rather than:
options: A move from one country to another

questions.question: Ortlund says Eastern Orthodoxy’s stance toward much of Protestantism is closer to “you’re not doing church” than to:
Ortlund points to which synod regarding claims about not having a valid Eucharist outside Eastern Orthodoxy?
Ortlund’s one-sentence thesis is that the stability and rigor people seek should be helped by finding them in:
options: The gospel of Jesus Christ—implying Protestant reform and renewal in practice
questions.answer: The gospel of Jesus Christ—implying Protestant reform and renewal in practice

questions.question: Ortlund associates much recent evangelical worship drift with:
options: Return to Gregorian chant in all churches
The seeker-sensitive movement’s normalization of an entertainment feel
questions.answer: The seeker-sensitive movement’s normalization of an entertainment feel

questions.question: A convert is quoted saying Orthodoxy “isn’t about us, it’s about” whom?
Ortlund’s pulpit-versus-stage anecdote is used to illustrate:
options: That all stage preaching is sinful
That pulpits are required for salvation
That changes often lack historical intentionality and should be made with awareness
questions.answer: That changes often lack historical intentionality and should be made with awareness

questions.question: He cites which awakening-era phenomenon to show he is not legalistic about pulpits?
options: The Second Great Awakening and controversial outdoor preaching
questions.answer: The Second Great Awakening and controversial outdoor preaching

questions.question: For the imagery of what endures forever versus grass that withers, Ortlund points to:
Ortlund uses Hebrews 10 to stress that through Christ believers have:
options: Access to God—an opened way contrasted with the old tabernacle restrictions
questions.answer: Access to God—an opened way contrasted with the old tabernacle restrictions

questions.question: He encourages those with intellectual angst to trust God for wisdom per:
Which seminary sponsors the video per Ortlund?
options: Oxford University Faculty of Theology only
